saññāpetha.gram: verb, imperative/optative 2nd person plural of saññāpeti (causative of sañjānāti, √ñā)
breakdown: Root “to know”; causative: to make known, convince.

saṁhareyya.meaning: should draw together; would collect, gather, pull in
saṁhareyya.gram: verb, optative 3rd person singular of saṁharati (saṁ + √har)

saṁhāni.meaning: decline; loss; running down (of the life faculties)
saṁhāni.note: divergence: the lexicon's running gloss reads “destroy, suppress” (as if from saṁhanati); the form here is the noun saṁhāni “decline”, used of āyuno saṁhāni, the running down of the life span.

saṁkelāyati.gram: verb, present tense 3rd person singular (causative of saṁkīḷati, √kīḷ)
breakdown: Root “to play”: to amuse, treat as a plaything.

saṁsandati.meaning: runs together; associates; agrees, fits together
breakdown: Flows (√sand). Together: flows together, converges, agrees.

saṁsandatimeva.meaning: just associates with; precisely agrees, comes together thus
saṁsandatimeva.gram: verb, present tense 3rd person singular + emphatic eva (sandhi)

saṁsara.meaning: wander on!; (imperative of saṁsarati) move through the round of existences
saṁsara.gram: verb, imperative 2nd person singular of saṁsarati (saṁ + √sar)
breakdown: Root meaning “to move, flow”: to wander on through births.
saṁsara.note: uncertain: the lexicon gives only the bare form saṁsara without a gloss; parsed here as a form of saṁsarati “to transmigrate”.

saṁsita.meaning: transmigrated; having moved about continuously (through the round of births)
saṁsita.gram: past participle of saṁsarati (contracted from saṁsarita)
breakdown: Root “to move, flow” with past participle suffix (saṁsarita > saṁsita).

saṁsitaṁ.gram: past participle of saṁsarati (accusative singular; contracted from saṁsarita)

saṁsārassatthikārako.meaning: the maker of the existence of this round of rebirths
breakdown: Of the round of existences (genitive singular of saṁsāra).
Root “to obstruct, ward off”: restraint, guarding.
saṁvaraṁ.goenka: Saṁvara, restraint, is the guarding of the sense doors and of one's conduct that protects the practice of sīla. The meditator restrains the senses not by suppression but with awareness, so that unwholesome states find no entry and the mind remains fit for samādhi and paññā.

saṁvattantī.meaning: they lead to; they conduce, involve
saṁvattantī.gram: verb, present tense 3rd person plural of saṁvattati
breakdown: Proceeds, goes on (√vatt). Together: leads to, conduces.

saṁvaḍḍhāni.gram: past participle of saṁvaḍḍhati (nominative/accusative plural neuter)
breakdown: Root “to grow” with past participle suffix: grown up.

saṁvejaniyesu.meaning: among (the places or things) that stir the heart; in those arousing a sense of urgency
breakdown: Root “to stir, tremble”; causative: to move, inspire urgency (saṁvega).

saṁvibhajati.gram: verb, present tense 3rd person singular (saṁ + vi + √bhaj)

saṁvibhajetha.meaning: should share; may (one) divide with others
saṁvibhajetha.gram: verb, optative (middle) 3rd person singular / 2nd person plural of saṁvibhajati

saṁvidahassu.meaning: arrange!; appoint, see to it (for yourself)
saṁvidahassu.gram: verb, imperative middle 2nd person singular of saṁvidahati (saṁ + vi + √dhā)

saṁvidhātuṁ.meaning: to arrange; to appoint, settle, organize
breakdown: Root “to place” with infinitive ending: to arrange.

saṁvijjanti.meaning: they are found, exist; (of persons) they are stirred, moved
saṁvijjanti.gram: verb, present tense 3rd person plural of saṁvijjati (√vid)
breakdown: Is found (passive of vindati, √vid); also “is stirred” (√vij).

saṁvijjantīdha.meaning: they are found here; there exist in this world
saṁvijjantīdha.gram: verb, present tense 3rd person plural + adverb idha (sandhi fusion)
breakdown: Here, in this world. (The -i and i- merge to -ī- in sandhi.)
Root “to ward off” with past participle suffix (vur + ta > vuta): restrained.
Root “to hold back” with noun suffix: restraint.

saṁyogena.meaning: by the bond; through the fetter, connection

breakdown: Root “to join” with past participle suffix: yoked, bound.

saṅgahappabhedaṁ.meaning: the divisions of the compilation; the classification by groupings
breakdown: Breaking up, division, classification (pa + √bhid). (The doubled pp is sandhi doubling.)

saṅgahitaparijanā.meaning: whose servants and attendants are kindly treated
breakdown: Kindly treated, taken care of (past participle of saṅgaṇhāti).
Root “to take hold” with noun suffix: taking care of, sympathy, support.

saṅgahāya.meaning: for the sake of kindness; for friendliness, sympathy, harmony
breakdown: Dative singular ending: “for the sake of”.
Root “to go” with abstract suffix: a coming together.

saṅgaṇhāti.meaning: takes care of; provides for, treats kindly, favours; collects
breakdown: Takes (√gah). Together: takes up, embraces, supports.

saṅghabhedasuttaṁ.meaning: the Sutta on the Splitting of the Saṅgha (Saṅghabhedasutta)
saṅghabhedasuttaṁ.gram: proper noun, neuter (title of a discourse)
breakdown: The Saṅgha, the community of monks.

saṅghānussatikathā, chaanussatiniddeso,.meaning: “The discussion of recollection of the Saṅgha”, in the Exposition of the Six Recollections, of the Visuddhimagga
breakdown: The discussion (kathā) of recollection (anussati) of the Saṅgha.
The exposition (niddesa) of the six (cha) recollections.

saṅghānussatikathā, visuddhimaggo..meaning: “The discussion of recollection of the Saṅgha”, in the Visuddhimagga
breakdown: The discussion (kathā) of recollection (anussati) of the Saṅgha.

saṅgāmavijayo.meaning: victory in battle; triumph in war

saṅgāravasuttaṁ.meaning: the Saṅgārava Sutta (Saṅgārava — name of a brahmin)
saṅgāravasuttaṁ.gram: proper noun, neuter (title of a discourse)
breakdown: Saṅgārava, a brahmin questioner of the Buddha.

saṅkappo.goenka: Saṅkappa, thought or intention, appears chiefly in sammāsaṅkappa, right thought — the second limb of the Noble Eightfold Path. Right thoughts are those of renunciation, of non-ill will and of non-violence; as the mind is purified by practice, thought inclines naturally away from craving and harm.

saṅkhalikhitaṁ.meaning: polished like a conch shell; carved and made smooth as shell (of the perfectly pure holy life)

saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharontīti.meaning: “…they put together what is conditioned” (end of quotation; why they are called saṅkhārā)
saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharontīti.gram: phrase: past participle (accusative) + verb 3rd person plural + quotation particle ti
breakdown: The conditioned, what is put together (past participle of saṅkharoti).
They condition, they put together (abhi + saṁ + √kar).
Quotation-closing particle (iti), with lengthening of the preceding vowel.
saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharontīti.goenka: This phrase comes from the canonical word-play explaining saṅkhāra: “they put together the conditioned, therefore they are called saṅkhārā”. The saṅkhāras are both the conditioning reactions and the conditioned products of those reactions — the mental habit patterns that vipassanā gradually brings to the surface and eradicates.

saṅkhyeyyapariveṇaṁ.meaning: the Saṅkhyeyya hermitage; the monastic cell named Saṅkhyeyya

saṅkhāra.meaning: mental conditioning; deep-rooted mentally conditioned reaction; volitional formation
saṅkhāra.summary: A central doctrinal term: the conditioned reactions of the mind, not “choices” or mere “formations”.
breakdown: Root meaning “to do, make”. Literally: a putting-together, a conditioning.
saṅkhāra.goenka: When the term saṅkhāra is used in Pāli, it usually refers to mental preconditioning or mentally conditioned reactions. Such reactions usually occur throughout one's life, mostly unnoticed below the threshold of consciousness. The Lekhasutta divides these reactive patterns into three categories: those of little influence that pass away quickly (like lines drawn on water), those of stronger influence (like lines in earth), and those of firm, predominant influence whose effect remains for a long period (like lines carved in rock). It is a helpful urge for every meditator to become more aware of the push inflicted by saṅkhāras and to exercise detachment in neutralising their strong impact. The canonical verse of the tradition (Hirīsutta, V1): anicca vata saṅkhārā, uppādavayadhammino, uppajjitvā nirujjhanti, tesaṁ vūpasamo sukho — “Impermanent truly are all saṅkhāras, by their nature constantly arising and vanishing. When they arise and are eradicated, their cessation brings true happiness.” The goal is visaṅkhāragataṁ cittaṁ — the mind become free of all saṅkhāras, totally purified.
saṅkhāra.note: divergence: academic translations sometimes render saṅkhāra as “choice” or “formation”; the lexicon's tradition-faithful sense is mental conditioning / conditioned reaction, and that sense is followed.

saṅkiyo.meaning: to be suspected; open to suspicion; anxious

saṇṭhapanā.meaning: composing; settling, deportment; setting in order
breakdown: Placing, setting (causative of √ṭhā “to stand”). Together: composing, settling.
